coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe based on monochromatic dual-emission carbon nanodots with fluorescence spectral change
coredata.dc:creator: Molla, Aniruddha
Lee, Hyein
Ju, Youngwon
Choi, Jeongyun
Kim, Joohoon
coredata.dc:description: We report a monochromatic dual-emission carbon nanodot (CD) as an unusual ratiometric fluorescence probe. The CD exhibits fluorescence spectral change from monochromatic blue to green emission, which is induced by Fe(III) ions as well as hydroxyl radicals. The monochromatic fluorescence spectral change of CD is utilized for a ratiometric fluorescence assay distinguishing Fe(III) and Fe(II) in aqueous media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Carbon nanodot
Monochromatic dual-emission
Fluorescence spectral change
Ferric ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs. Insets show a HR-TEM image (upper left) and a size-distribution histogram (upper right). (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs and starting precursor material m-PD.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs (black solid) and m-PD (black dotted); normalized PL excitation (solid yellow) and emission spectra (solid blue) of CDs. λ em = 405 nm and λ ex = 345 nm. Inset shows a photograph of CDs in ethanol under UV irradiation (λ max = 365 nm). (b) Normalized PL emission spectra of CDs at different λ ex from 295 to 365 nm. Ethanol as a solvent.

(a) PL emission spectra of CDs before (blue) and after (black) adding Fenton's reagent. Inset shows an enlarged PL emission spectrum after adding Fenton's reagent. λ ex = 345 nm (b) PL emission spectra of CDs at different λ ex from 345 to 480 nm after adding Fenton's reagent. Ethanol as a solvent.

PL emission spectra (solid lines) of CDs at (a) λ ex = 460 nm and (b) λ ex = 345 nm in the presence of thiourea after adding Fenton's reagent. For comparison, PL emission spectrum (dashed blue line) of only CDs at λ ex = 345 nm before adding Fenton's reagent is presented in part (b). Fenton's reagent: 2.5 mM H2O2 and 1.18 mM Fe(II). Insets show bar plots of PL intensity. All of PL emission spectra were normalized and the error bars represent the standard deviations derived from independent measurements (n = 3). Ethanol and water as a solvent for CDs and the rest of the reagents, respectively.

PL emission spectra (solid lines) of CDs at (a) λ ex = 460 nm and (b) λ ex = 345 nm in the presence of 1.18 mM Fe(III) before and after adding EDTA. For comparison, PL emission spectrum (dashed blue line) of only CDs at λ ex = 345 nm in the absence of Fe(III) is presented in part (b). Insets shows bar plots of PL intensity. All of PL emission spectra were normalized and the error bars represent the standard deviations derived from independent measurements (n = 3). Ethanol and water as a solvent for CDs and the rest of the reagents, respectively.

PL spectral changes of CDs with the addition of Fe(III). The solid and dashed lines are the PL spectra of CDs at λ ex = 345 nm and λ ex = 460 nm, respectively. Inset shows PL intensity ratio at 510 nm and 405 nm (i.e. I510/I405) depending on the concentration of Fe(III) and Fe(II). λ ex = 460 nm for I510 and λ ex = 345 nm for I405. Aqueous ethanol (ethanol:H2O = 1:1 v/v) as a solvent.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe based on monochromatic dual-emission carbon nanodots with fluorescence spectral change

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute for Basic Sciences, KHU-KIST Department of Converging Science and Technology, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: KHU-KIST Department of Converging Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute for Basic Sciences, KHU-KIST Department of Converging Science and Technology, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Education and Research Center for Smart Energy and Materials, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a monochromatic dual-emission carbon nanodot (CD) as an unusual ratiometric fluorescence probe. The CD exhibits fluorescence spectral change from monochromatic blue to green emission, which is induced by Fe(III) ions as well as hydroxyl radicals. The monochromatic fluorescence spectral change of CD is utilized for a ratiometric fluorescence assay distinguishing Fe(III) and Fe(II) in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:para: As a unique class of fluorescent materials, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention because of their interesting optical features and ease of synthesis [1–3]. Furthermore, CDs are considered to be a potential alternative to traditional fluorescent materials such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in wide applications due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and low cost [4–7]. In particular, CDs have been effectively used for fluorescence sensing applications, and the most common sensing principle in these applications is based on fluorescence intensity changes of CDs (i.e., fluorescence quenching or enhancement) in the presence of analytes [8,9]. However, this single intensity-based sensing is often compromised by unknown external interferences [10]. Ratiometric fluorescence assays are based on the ratio of dual fluorescence-emission intensities at different wavelengths, and thus they provide more reliable analysis than do single intensity-based fluorescence assays [11,12]. Accordingly, substantial efforts have been made to utilize fluorescent CDs as ratiometric fluorescence probes [13–17]. In CD-based ratiometric fluorescence probes, the CDs often require additional fluorophores that act as reference signals, response signals, and energy acceptors [18–21]. Recently, a new type of ratiometric fluorescence CD probes has been reported with dichromatic fluorescence emissions at two different wavelengths for use as reference and response signals. For example, ratiometric fluorescence pH sensing was demonstrated utilizing the dual emissions of CDs synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 3-aminophenylboronic acid, citric acid/basic fuchsin, or ascorbic acid [22,23]. A ratiometric fluorescence thermometer was also reported using sucrose-derived CDs whose dual emissions declined with the increase of temperature while one of the dual emissions declined faster [24]. In addition, multi-functional ratiometric fluorescence CDs were demonstrated for detecting multiple targets including pH, temperature, and cationic ions such as Al(III) or Fe(III) [25,26]. These ratiometric fluorescence CDs exhibit unique dual emissions that do not require additional fluorophores for ratiometric sensing and thus offer new opportunities for ratiometric fluorescence assays. However, trace impurities in CDs are of concern as a potential source of one of the dichromatic emissions, and thus purifying synthesized CDs is critical [27].

In this study, we report a monochromatic dual-emission CD that exhibited fluorescence spectral change as a ratiometric fluorescence probe. Specifically, the as-prepared CDs displayed a monochromatic blue emission with a maximum peak at 405 nm. However, we found that the fluorescent CDs unexpectedly generated a monochromatic green emission with a main peak at 510 nm upon the addition of Fenton's reagent, but the original blue emission was completely quenched. Detailed experimental studies indicated that the CDs' emission change from blue to green was ascribed to Fe(III) as well as HO•. This discovery is significant because it allows the use of CDs as an unusual ratiometric fluorescence probe with monochromatic dual-emission for selective discrimination of Fe(III) over Fe(II) and other metal ions in aqueous samples beneficially without requiring any additional fluorophores, and thus opens up a new potential use of fluorescent CDs in metal ion-related sensing applications.

The following chemicals were received from Sigma Aldrich, Inc. (USA): m-phenylenediamine (m-PD, 99%), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3•6H2O, 98%), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4•7H2O, 99%), gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4•3H2O, 99.9%), silver(I) nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9%), copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4, 99.9%), nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2•6H2O, 98.5%), cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2•6H2O, 98%), cadmium(II) nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2•4H2O, 99.997%), thiourea (SC(NH2)2, 99.0%), sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl, available chlorine 10–15%), potassium superoxide (KO2, powder), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99.4–100.6%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99%), and quinine sulfate (QY = 55% in 0.1 M H2SO4). Ethyl acetate (EtOAc, 99.5%), hexane (n-C6H14, 95%), ethanol (EtOH, 95%), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 34.5 wt% in H2O) were purchased from Samchun Chemical Co., Ltd. (Korea). Lead(II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, 97.0%) was purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Korea). Silica gel 60 (mesh size 230–400) was brought from Merck KGaA (Germany) whereas anhydrous zinc(II) chloride (ZnCl2, 99.95%) and aluminium(III) chloride (AlCl3, 99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar-Thermo Fisher Scientific, PLC. (USA) and Acros Organics-Thermo Fisher Scientific, PLC. (USA), respectively. These chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Deionized water (DI water, 18 MΩ•cm) was used in the preparation of aqueous solutions (Ultra370 and Aquapuri 5, Younglin Co., Korea).

CDs were synthesized via microwave treatment of aqueous m-PD solution and subsequent purification using silica gel column chromatography. Briefly, 0.250 g of m-PD was dissolved in 10 mL of DI water in 30 mL reaction vial (Reaction Vial G30, Anton Paar GmbH., Austria), and then the aqueous solution was subjected to a microwave irradiation cycle, including heating to 220 °C, maintaining for 10 min, and cooling down to 55 °C, in a microwave synthesis reactor (Monowave 300, Anton Paar GmbH., Austria) under magnetic stirring. After removing water using a rotatory evaporator, the crude CDs were purified by silica gel (230–400 mesh) column chromatography using mixtures of ethyl acetate and hexane as eluents. After removing solvents and further drying, the purified CDs were dispersed in ethanol (0.25 mg/mL). Yield of the final purified sample was ∼5 wt%.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin instrument (FEI Co., USA) in the Center for Advanced Functional Ceramics (Gachon University, Korea) at an operating voltage of 300 kV. TEM samples were prepared by placing a drop of CDs solution on a 400 mesh carbon-coated copper grid (Ted Pella Inc., USA) and allowing the grid to dry in air. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Alpha-II FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Co., USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000–600 cm−1 in ATR mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with a K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA) using Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). XPS samples were prepared by dropping CDs solutions on Si wafers and allowing them to dry in air. XPS peak fitting was carried out using the Fittwin software (non-commercialized fitting software, ESCALAB, Seoul National University). UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded using an Agilent 8453 UV–Vis spectrometer (Agilent Tech., USA) and a FluoroMate FS-2 fluorescence spectrometer (Scinco Co., Korea), respectively. The optical path length of quartz cuvettes was 1 cm. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were acquired using a Fluorolog®-3 spectrofluorometer system equipped with a time-correlated single-photon counter (TCSPC) (Horiba Scientific, Ltd., Japan). Zeta potential measurements were carried out with a Malvern Nano-ZS90 instrument (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., UK) using water as solvent at room temperature. Quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QY = 55% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference according to the following equation:
Φ S = Φ Ref * (A Ref/I Ref) * (I S/A S) * (η S 2/η Ref 2)
where Φ is the QY, I is the integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.36 for ethanol), and A is the optical density. The subscript “S” and “Ref” stand for samples and references (i.e., quinine sulfate), respectively.

Typical ratiometric fluorescence assays were performed as follows. 50 μL of CDs solution (0.25 mg/mL in ethanol) was added to 2 mL of ethanol in a 5 mL sample vial and followed by the addition of aqueous Fe(II) solution (final concentration: 1.18 mM) and aqueous H2O2 solution (final concentration: 2.5 mM). After stirring the mixture solution at room temperature for 10 min, fluorescence measurements were performed immediately at excitation wavelengths of 345 nm and 460 nm. For ratiometric fluorescence assays of Fe(III) and other metal ions such as Fe(II), Ag(I), Cu(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Al(III), and Au(III), aqueous Fe(III) or other metal ion solutions at different concentrations were added into the aqueous ethanol CDs solutions (0.25 mg/mL in aqueous ethanol (ethanol:H2O = 1:1 v/v)) as described above but without adding the aqueous Fe(II) and H2O2 solutions in the typical ratiometric fluorescence assays. For scavenging experiments of HO• radicals, aqueous Fe(II) solution (final concentration: 1.18 mM) and aqueous H2O2 solution (final concentration: 2.5 mM) were sequentially added to 2 mL of ethanol containing thiourea at different concentrations (i.e., final concentrations: 5, 10, and 15 mM). The mixture solution was allowed to react at room temperature for 10 min under stirring. After adding 50 μL of CDs solution (0.25 mg/mL in ethanol) to the mixture solution, the reaction mixture was stirred additionally for 10 min. Fluorescence measurements were then carried out immediately. To study the effect of 1O2 and O2 •- on the fluorescence spectral change of CDs, a mixture of NaOCl (final concentration: 2.5 mM) and H2O2 (final concentration: 2.5 mM), and KO2 (final concentration: 2.5 mM) were used, respectively, instead of Fenton's reagent. For photoluminescence (PL) reversal experiments via EDTA chelation of Fe(III), aqueous EDTA solution (final concentration: 11.8 mM) containing Na2CO3 (final concentration: 23.6 mM) was added to the CDs solution after completion of the fluorescence spectral changes of CDs in the presence of Fenton's reagent (final concentrations: 2.5 mM H2O2 and 1.18 mM Fe(II)) or Fe(III) (final concentration: 1.18 mM). The reaction mixture was stirred additionally up to 120 min. Fluorescence measurements were then performed immediately.

We prepared fluorescent CDs via microwave treatment of aqueous m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) solution for 10 min at 220 °C and then purified the CDs using silica gel column chromatography (Experimental Section for details). Fig. 1 a shows TEM images and the corresponding size-distribution histogram of the CDs, representing well-dispersed amorphous CDs with an average size of 19.8 (±3.9) nm. We also conducted FT-IR measurements to characterize the chemical structure of the CDs. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of the starting precursor m-PD, as shown in Fig. 1b, some new characteristic FT-IR peaks emerged at 2957-2850 cm−1, 1722 cm−1, and 1591-1510 cm−1 for the CDs, which were ascribed to aliphatic C–H, CO, and CN stretching vibrations, respectively [28,29]. A similar FT-IR spectrum was reported recently for fluorescent CDs prepared by solvothermal treatment of m-PD at 180 °C for 12 h, which suggested that decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions had occurred during the formation of CDs via the microwave treatment of m-PD in the same way as the CDs prepared previously via the solvothermal treatment of m-PD [28]. XPS studies further verified the chemical structure of the CDs (ESI†, Figs. S1 and S2); the wide-scan XPS spectrum of the CDs (ESI†, Fig. S1) confirmed their elemental composition (i.e., C, N, and O), and their deconvoluted XPS spectra (ESI†, Fig. S2) verified their chemical bonds consistent with the FT-IR data.

To investigate the optical properties of the CDs, we performed UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies of the CDs. Fig. 2 a shows the UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation, and PL emission spectra of the CDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs displayed two distinct absorption bands at 289 nm and 349 nm compared to those of the starting material m-PD at 245 nm and 294 nm (Fig. 2a). The band at 289 nm was ascribable to the π-π* transition of the CN bond in the aromatic sp2 domains of the CDs [30]. The other transition at 349 nm was attributed to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states of the CDs [31]. In comparison to the starting material m-PD, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs also exhibited red-shifts of the electronic absorption transitions from 245 nm to 289 nm and from 294 nm to 349 nm, indicating that the CDs had smaller electronic band gaps than m-PD [28]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, the CDs exhibited bright blue PL under UV illumination while the starting material m-PD did not (ESI†, inset Fig. S3). The maximum emission and excitation wavelengths of the CDs were 405 nm and 345 nm, respectively (Fig. 2a). Unlike many other CDs reported previously [32,33], the emission wavelength of the CDs in the present study rarely depended on the excitation wavelengths ranging from 295 nm to 365 nm (Fig. 2b), which indicated uniformity in the luminescent surface states of the CDs [34]. The optimal excitation wavelength for intense blue PL emission was 345 nm, and the PL quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was determined to be 11.9% at λ ex = 345 nm with quinine sulfate as a PLQY standard (ESI†, Fig. S4). This PLQY was higher than that (i.e., 4.8%) of similar CDs prepared previously via the solvothermal treatment of m-PD [28]. We also conducted time-resolved PL spectroscopy of the CDs, revealing mono-exponential PL decay with a lifetime of 2.53 ns (ESI†, Fig. S5, and Table S1).

However, as shown in Fig. 3 a, the blue PL emission of the CDs was significantly quenched with the addition of Fenton's reagent (i.e., H2O2 and Fe(II)). The quenching process was fast and completed in less than 10 min (ESI†, Fig. S6a). Interestingly, in addition to quenching the blue PL, adding Fenton's reagent generated weak green PL emission at ca. 500 nm (inset, Fig. 3a). The CDs' new green PL intensity depended on excitation wavelengths ranging from 345 nm to 480 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength was 460 nm for maximum green PL emission intensity at 510 nm, while the emission wavelength was fairly independent on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). As with the quenching process, the new green PL emission of the CDs was achieved within 10 min with the added Fenton's reagent (ESI†, Fig. S6b). This was an interesting finding because adding Fenton's reagent led to the bright green PL emission of the CDs (i.e., ∼56 times greater than the negligible green emission of the as-prepared CDs (ESI†, Fig. S6b)) as well as the total quenching of the original blue PL emission.

To gain insight into the fluorescence spectral change of CDs from monochromatic blue to green emission with the addition of Fenton's reagent, we performed a set of key control experiments. Because Fenton's reagent is a solution of H2O2 with Fe(II) ion [35], we investigated the effect of H2O2 on the PL properties of the CDs. In the absence of Fe(II), there was a negligible effect of H2O2 on the generation of the new green PL emission of the CDs as well as on the quenching of their original blue PL emission (ESI†, Fig. S7a). In contrast, in the presence of Fe(II), the fluorescence spectral change of the CDs was linearly dependent on the concentration of H2O2 from 0.1 to 2.5 mM (ESI†, Fig. S7b). These results indicate that the CDs' fluorescence spectral change requires not only H2O2 but also Fe(II) ions. Therefore, we further investigated the role of Fe(II) ions in the fluorescence spectral change of the CDs. Interestingly, and similar to the case with H2O2 alone, adding only Fe(II) did not lead to the significant spectral change (ESI†, Fig. S8).

Because Fe(II) is oxidized to Fe(III) by H2O2 while producing HO• in Fenton's reagent [35], we hypothesized that HO• and Fe(III) ions were critical to the CDs' fluorescence spectral change. First, to test the hypothesis experimentally, we performed quenching experiments of the new green PL emission using thiourea as a specific quencher for HO•. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the presence of thiourea significantly inhibited the generation of the CDs' green PL emission even after the addition of Fenton's reagent; the inhibition also increased considerably with increasing concentrations of thiourea. These results clearly indicated that the generation of green PL emission was attributed to the HO• formed in Fenton's reagent. However, the generation of green PL emission of the CDs was not fully suppressed even with thiourea concentration increased to 15 mM (inset in Fig. 4a). In addition, we found that the quenched blue PL emission of the CDs that was induced with Fenton's reagent was barely recovered even with high concentrations of thiourea for effective scavenging of HO• (Fig. 4b). All of these results suggested that HO• was critical to generating the CDs' new green PL emission, but other species formed from Fenton's reagent also played a vital role in generating the green PL emission and especially in quenching the original blue emission. We thus tested the effects of other reactive oxygen species that might be present in Fenton's reagent such as 1O2 and O2 •- on generating the green PL emission in the CDs. However, neither 1O2 nor O2 •- was able to generate the new green PL or to quench the original blue PL emission of the CDs (ESI†, Fig. S9). Finally, we verified the hypothesis of the critical role of Fe(III) on the fluorescence spectral change of the CDs. Indeed, we found that Fe(III) alone quenched the CDs' original blue PL emission and generated the new green PL in the same way as did Fenton’ reagent (ESI†, Fig. S10), which indicated the indispensable role of Fe(III) in the fluorescence spectral change of the CDs in Fenton's reagent.

To better comprehend the crucial role of Fe(III) in the fluorescence spectral change of the CDs, we reversed the Fe(III)-induced fluorescence spectral change of CDs via EDTA chelation of Fe(III). As shown in Fig. 5 a, the new Fe(III)-induced green PL was suppressed with the addition of EDTA, with decreasing green PL intensity with increasing EDTA treatment time. Similarly, the Fe(III)-induced quenching of the original blue PL of CDs was recovered with the EDTA treatment, and the recovered blue intensity increased with longer treatment times (Fig. 5b). We also observed the PL recovery of the blue emission with Fenton's reagent when treating with EDTA (ESI†, Fig. S11), but the recovery extent was smaller, mainly because of the additional quenching process with HO• as well as Fe(III) generated in Fenton's reagent. All of these results clearly verified the hypothesis, indicating that the fluorescence spectral change of the CDs originated from HO• and Fe(III).

To understand the process of how HO• and Fe(III) induce the fluorescence spectral change of CDs, we further characterized the CDs treated with HO• (i.e., Fenton's reagent) and Fe(III). XPS measurements of CDs treated with Fenton's reagent showed the increased oxygen-to-carbon (O/C %) ratio of the CDs compared with that of the as-prepared CDs (i.e., from 10.2 ± 6.2% to 64.2 ± 19.3%). We ascribed this to HO• radicals formed in Fenton's reagent leading to the change in the CDs' oxidation states due to the oxidative HO• radicals [36]. Similarly, Ding et al. recently reported that the increased oxidation degree in CDs reduced the band gap and thus led to a red-shift in their PL emission wavelengths [30]. In addition, zeta potential measurements of CDs revealed that zeta potential value of the CDs changed dramatically from −12.4 mV to +12.6 mV after the treatment of the CDs with Fe(III). This suggested strong coordination of Fe(III) ions with oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface, which presumably altered the CDs' electronic states, not only accelerating the non-radiative relaxation pathways but also disturbing the energy levels of the CDs' excited states [37,38]. Therefore, the fluorescence spectral change of CDs was ascribable to the physio-chemical processes in which HO• radicals modified the CDs' oxidation states and Fe(III) ions altered their electronic states via the CDs' oxidation and the strong coordination of Fe(III) on CDs, respectively.

Taking advantage of the monochromatic dual-emission CDs that displayed the fluorescence spectral change, we demonstrated the feasible use of the CDs as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for selective discrimination of Fe(III) over Fe(II) and other metal ions in aqueous media. Because Fe(III) selectively induced the monochromatic green PL emission from the CDs whose fluorescence was originally monochromatic blue, we expected the CDs' fluorescence spectral change to be utilized for a ratiometric fluorescence assay of Fe(III) even without requiring additional fluorophores. Fig. 6 presents the CDs' fluorescence spectral changes with Fe(III) added at different concentrations, with distinctive linear trends apparent. The inset of Fig. 6 (i.e., I510/I405 where I510 and I405 indicate the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm and 405 nm, respectively) clearly shows that the fluorescence spectral change increased linearly with two different slopes (i.e., 15.54 ppm−1 (R2 = 0.972) and 35.00 ppm−1 (R2 = 0.997)) upon the addition of Fe(III) while adding Fe(II) resulted in nearly negligible change. This indicates unambiguous ratiometric differentiation of Fe(III) over Fe(II) with the monochromatic fluorescence spectral change of the CDs. The limit of detection of Fe(III) was determined to be 0.17 ppm (k = 3). In particular, to emphasize the performance of the ratiometric fluorescence assay with conventional single PL intensity-based assay, we also extended our selectivity test of the CDs with other typical metal ions (i.e., Ag(I), Cu(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Al(III), and Au(III)). As expected, adding Fe(III) markedly quenched the CDs' blue PL emission at 405 nm, while other metal ions including Fe(II), Ag(I), Cu(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) had comparably minimal effects on demolishing blue emission even at twice the concentration of the Fe(III) (ESI†, Fig. S12a). Nevertheless, as with Fe(III), Au(III) completely quenched the CDs' blue emission, and Al(III) also showed a substantial quenching effect. This indicates that the conventional single blue PL intensity-based sensing of Fe(III) could be compromised by Au(III) and Al(III) (ESI†, Fig. S12a). However, in terms of generating the CDs' new green PL emission, only Fe(III) was effective while other metal ions had a negligible effect on generating the green emission (ESI†, Fig. S12b). This finding clearly demonstrates the benefits of the CDs’ unique fluorescence spectral change from monochromatic blue to green emission, allowing the unusual ratiometric fluorescence assay to distinguish Fe(III) from other metal ions including Fe(II) in aqueous media.

In summary, we reported a monochromatic dual-emission CDs exhibiting fluorescence spectral change turned on with Fe(III) selectively over other metal ions including Fe(II) in aqueous media as a unique ratiometric fluorescence probe. The CDs were prepared via microwave treatment of aqueous m-PD and subsequent purification with silica gel column chromatography. The CDs showed unexpected fluorescence spectral change from monochromatic blue to green emission, which was induced by Fe(III) as well as HO•. Based on the unique monochromatic dual-emission nature of CDs, we applied the CDs as an unusual ratiometric fluorescence probe requiring no additional fluorophores for selective detection of Fe(III) over Fe(II) and other metal ions in aqueous media, which demonstrated a new conceptual advance in ratiometric fluorescence sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea grant funded by the Korean government (Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning) (Grant No.: NRF-2020R1A2C2013790, NRF-2021R1A4A5032876, and NRF-2019M3E6A1065038).
